story:
He walked into the night with a gas lamp in one hand and a garden fork in the other. The moon hung low over fields of linseed, the stalks whispering in the breeze. He pushed into the woods, the lamp held out before him. An oak creaked to his right. He moved carefully down the slope, threading between branches, listening past the lamp's hiss. He sniffed. He was sure he could smell it, whatever it was.

He turned the lamp off and set it down. In the dark, the forest opened to sound. A faint buzzing floated in the air. Fork raised before him, feet testing each step, he made his way downhill until the trees gave way to the next field. The buzzing drew closer. He planted the fork in the ground and held the wooden handle with delicate fingers, feeling for vibrations that matched the distant hum. He turned his head, listened, sniffed. There. He found the line of it.

He untied his shoes and set them down. He peeled off his socks and flung them into the mud, letting his bare feet sink into the damp earth. A quick thrill ran through him. He ran. He shot across the field, grass slapping his ankles, coat slipping from his shoulders. The moon hovered just above the horizon. He stared at it and kept going.

As he ran, he tugged off his sweater, then his T-shirt, nearly stumbling but never falling. He tossed them aside and slowed to a jog, then to a halt at the edge of a gully. He stripped off jeans and underwear, dropped his watch on top of them, and climbed down, as bare as the sky above. He stood in the thin stream that cut along the gully's middle. Here, the buzzing was loudest. It seemed to fill the air itself, its source nowhere and everywhere.

He tipped his head back to the stars overhead. The hum grew stronger, a pressure he could feel in his bones, a shiver that ran through his skin and deeper. The sound climbed, blooming into the whole night. A brief flash.

He was gone.

The wind moved his scattered clothes where they lay in a trail across the field. Down in the gully, at the place he had stood, only one thing remained: a sloughed outer layer of skin, a light, bedraggled shell resting on the ground, as if something had stepped out of it and left. Somewhere far above the field, beyond the trees and the linseed and the stream, he traveled on.
